note: Authored without reading families.json, questions.json, blind-corpus.json, or engine code. Fallacy definitions (teaching + pick_example) only. For routing measurement.

cases.label: ad_hominem
cases.argument: So here is what happened. My neighbor Sarah brought up at the HOA meeting that the playground equipment is rusting and needs replacing before a kid gets hurt. Instead of anyone addressing the actual safety issue, Kevin immediately jumps in with 'Sarah, you've lived here less than two years and you don't even have children. Why should we listen to you about playgrounds?' The rust is visible to anyone with eyes. Whether she has kids or just moved in has nothing to do with whether the equipment is dangerous. But the whole conversation shifted to defending Sarah's right to speak instead of the rusted bolts.

cases.label: ad_hominem
cases.argument: During our sprint retrospective, the junior developer pointed out that our CI pipeline has been running integration tests against a stale database snapshot for at least three weeks, meaning several merged PRs passed tests that would have failed against production data. Our tech lead dismissed it with 'You've only been on this team for six months, so you probably don't understand that we intentionally pinned that snapshot version for stability reasons.' The problem is, whether intentional or not, testing against stale data defeats the purpose of the integration suite. The developer's tenure has nothing to do with whether the database snapshot is current.

cases.label: ad_hominem
cases.argument: You: 'I think the company should review its remote work policy. Productivity is down across three departments since the mandate.' Your manager: 'You've been late to standup four times this month and missed the Q2 deadline by a week. Maybe focus on your own performance before lecturing leadership about policy.' The manager never addresses the productivity data across three departments. Your punctuality doesn't change whether the numbers are accurate. It's a complete sidestep of the actual claim.

cases.label: strawman
cases.argument: Consider the following. The city councilmember proposed that the police department budget be held flat for one fiscal year while an independent audit reviews overtime spending patterns, which have increased forty percent in two years. The police union's response: 'Councilmember Chen wants to defund the police and leave our neighborhoods without protection.' But holding a budget flat is not defunding. It means spending the same amount. And an audit is not abolition. It's reviewing where money goes. The union defeated a position nobody actually took while the real proposal about overtime transparency went unanswered.

cases.label: strawman
cases.argument: At the hospital pharmacy committee, I recommended we add a mandatory second-pharmacist verification step for high-risk IV compounded medications, citing two recent near-miss events in our own facility and updated ISMP guidelines from last year. The director of nursing responded: 'Dr. Patel wants to slow down every IV delivery in the hospital. Patients in the ICU can't wait an extra forty minutes for their drips while pharmacists double-check each other's math.' I never proposed verifying every IV. Only high-risk compounded preparations, which represent roughly five percent of all IV orders. The director argued against a fiction.

cases.label: strawman
cases.argument: I agree with the environmental working group that carbon emissions need to come down, and I support the renewable energy targets they've laid out. But when they say we should phase out natural gas peaker plants over the next decade, my colleagues in the opposition immediately respond as if the group proposed shutting down the entire electrical grid tomorrow and sending us back to candles and wood stoves. Nobody said anything about eliminating baseload power. The actual proposal includes a detailed transition plan with battery storage milestones and grid modernization timelines. Argue against that, not the apocalypse fantasy.

cases.label: tu_quoque
cases.argument: You: 'You really need to stop interrupting people during meetings. It shuts down discussion and several team members have mentioned it to me privately.' Me: 'Oh, that's rich coming from you. You cut Marcus off twice in the all-hands last month, and I'm pretty sure Elena was mid-sentence when you jumped in during the design review. So maybe clean up your own act before policing mine.' The question is whether my interruptions are a problem, not whether you've also interrupted people. My behavior, even if identical, doesn't make your interruptions any less disruptive.

cases.label: tu_quoque
cases.argument: The mayor held a press conference calling for ethics reform after three city council members were caught steering contracts to relatives. Within hours, one of those council members released a statement: 'The mayor accepted a five-hundred-dollar dinner from a developer two years ago and didn't report it until the newspaper asked about it. So he has no standing to lecture anyone about ethics.' A five-hundred-dollar unreported dinner is a real problem, but it doesn't make contract-steering acceptable. The council member answered a corruption charge by pointing at a disclosure violation. Two different scales, and the original allegation remains unanswered.

cases.label: tu_quoque
cases.argument: Your accounting firm flagged eighteen material weaknesses in our client's internal controls over financial reporting, recommending a full remediation plan before the next audit cycle. The CFO shot back: 'Your own firm paid a two-million-dollar fine to the SEC for audit independence violations three years ago, so spare me the lecture on controls.' Whether the auditing firm itself had a regulatory issue is separate from whether the eighteen material weaknesses actually exist in this client's ICFR. One doesn't cancel out the other. The balance sheet doesn't care about the auditor's past.

cases.label: poisoning_the_well
cases.argument: I normally believe in hearing from all stakeholders before making a decision, and I respect that Dr. Fischer has published extensively on vaccine policy. But before you listen to her presentation today, you should know that her research institute has accepted over twelve million dollars from a naturopathic supplement manufacturer with a direct financial interest in undermining vaccination requirements. I'm not saying her data is wrong, but you should keep that funding in mind. Actually, just keep it in mind before she even starts speaking. Her conclusions are probably already aligned with her donors' interests rather than the science.

cases.label: poisoning_the_well
cases.argument: So the board meeting was about to start, and right before the former CEO was scheduled to present his analysis of our new strategy, the current chair leaned over and said to everyone: 'Look, David is going to give you a very polished presentation with charts and projections. Just remember, this is the same guy who missed revenue targets for seven straight quarters and left us with a seventy-million-dollar lawsuit. Take everything he says with a grain of salt.' Then David walked in and the chair smiled and said 'David, great to see you.' Nobody at that table could hear David's actual numbers with fresh ears after that setup. The well was thoroughly poisoned.

cases.label: poisoning_the_well
cases.argument: Manager: 'Alright team, Lisa from the design department is going to walk us through the new UI mockups. Fair warning though. Lisa has opposed every single change we've proposed in the last year, so just be prepared for another round of negativity and obstruction. Go ahead, Lisa.' Lisa: 'Actually, I wanted to show some data from user testing that supports...' Team: [already checked out]. This is the textbook setup. The manager didn't argue against anything Lisa might say. He just labeled her before she could speak so anything she offers arrives pre-rejected.

cases.label: genetic_fallacy
cases.argument: Consider the following chain of reasoning. The proposal for a municipal broadband network comes from a policy paper authored by a think tank that also receives funding from a major telecom lobby. Because that think tank has taken telecom money, the municipal broadband proposal must be flawed and self-serving. But this line of thought never examines the actual proposal. The cost estimates, the coverage maps, the comparisons to cities like Chattanooga that built successful public networks. The origin of an idea can be a red flag worth investigating, but it doesn't constitute a refutation. A sound idea from a compromised source is still sound.

cases.label: genetic_fallacy
cases.argument: The surgical protocol for laparoscopic cholecystectomy that Dr. Nakamura is advocating was first developed at a hospital in Thailand. Because the techniques originated in a country with different regulatory standards than ours, we should not adopt them. This conclusion jumps from origin directly to dismissal without evaluating the protocol on its own terms. The complication rates in the published case series, the randomized trial data from three independent centers, or the fact that the American College of Surgeons has already reviewed and endorsed a substantially similar version. Where it was first developed is not the same as whether it works.

cases.label: genetic_fallacy
cases.argument: You: 'Check out this analysis showing that corporate tax cuts in Kansas led to slower GDP growth than neighboring states that kept their rates.' Me: 'That study was published by the Center for Budget and Policy Priorities, which is a left-leaning organization. Anything they produce is going to be slanted, so the whole analysis is probably cherry-picked.' The political lean of the publisher doesn't tell us whether the data was fabricated, whether the methodology was solid, or whether the comparison states were fairly chosen. You deal with the study itself, not the address of the organization that published it.

cases.label: false_dilemma
cases.argument: You keep saying you're unhappy with the new product roadmap. Fine. You have two choices: get on board with the direction we've set, or find a different team whose roadmap you can support. Those are the only options here. We're not going to re-litigate six months of planning because one senior engineer has reservations, and we're certainly not going to maintain two parallel roadmaps. But there are actually more options. Suggesting modifications at the next quarterly review, running a limited pilot of an alternative approach, documenting concerns formally for the steering committee, or proposing a phased rollout that tests assumptions. This frames dissent as quitting.

cases.label: false_dilemma
cases.argument: The school board meeting was down to the wire. The budget gap was two-point-four million dollars. The superintendent put up two slides: one showed cutting all middle school sports, the other showed eliminating the instrumental music program district-wide. 'We have to pick one,' she said, 'because we're out of time and these are the only programs large enough to close the gap in a single cut.' A parent raised her hand and asked about reducing the administrative travel budget, freezing superintendent-level salary increases, and deferring the planned stadium renovation. Together those added up to two-point-six million. The superintendent said those had already been 'ruled out.' By whom, she never said.

cases.label: false_dilemma
cases.argument: In treating your chronic hypertension, you essentially have two paths: start a daily ACE inhibitor regimen, or accept the progressive cardiovascular risks associated with uncontrolled Stage 2 hypertension, including a significantly elevated chance of myocardial infarction or stroke within the next decade. There isn't a neutral third option here. But this ignores lifestyle interventions like dietary sodium reduction, structured exercise programs, stress management protocols, weight loss, and limiting alcohol. All of which are first-line recommendations in the AHA/ACC guidelines before or alongside pharmacotherapy. The doctor presented medication versus catastrophe as if nothing else existed.

cases.label: false_equivalence
cases.argument: Let's examine what each side did wrong. The city council approved a permit application that omitted a required environmental impact addendum, which delayed the project by three weeks and cost the developer about forty thousand dollars in holding costs. Meanwhile, the developer's CEO was convicted of bribing two state regulators with envelopes of cash totaling one hundred eighty thousand dollars to fast-track approvals on unrelated projects. Both sides clearly made mistakes in the permitting process, so it seems fair to say there's blame to go around on all sides. A bureaucratic paperwork omission is not remotely equivalent to felony bribery. Equating them erases the difference in scale, intent, and illegality.

cases.label: false_equivalence
cases.argument: I agree that both major-party candidates have made statements they probably regret, and I understand why voters find the whole thing exhausting. But treating the candidate who exaggerated the attendance numbers at three rallies the same as the candidate who knowingly spread election fraud claims that led to the certification hearing being stormed. Those are not comparable things. One is standard political puffery. The other is a direct assault on the democratic transfer of power. When we say 'both sides have their problems,' we flatten a qualitative difference into a quantitative one, and the comparison itself becomes dishonest.

cases.label: false_equivalence
cases.argument: My coworker told me this story like it proved some deep point. 'My daughter got suspended for three days because she shared her prescribed Adderall with a friend who was struggling to study. Meanwhile, the kid who sold opioids in the parking lot got the same three-day suspension. Same punishment for both. That's how you know the system is broken. They treat all drugs the same.' But the issue isn't whether the school treats all drugs the same. Sharing prescription stimulants is a federal crime, and the school's discipline code doesn't require a graduated scale based on street value. Equating those two situations because they got the same consequence misses that they're fundamentally different offenses with different legal frameworks.

cases.label: perfect_solution
cases.argument: The proposed patch fixes the SQL injection vulnerability in the login endpoint by parameterizing all user-supplied input in the authentication module. But it doesn't address the same pattern in the search API, the user profile update endpoint, or the reporting export function. If we're going to ship a security fix, we should do it comprehensively. Otherwise we're just giving ourselves a false sense of security. Let's hold this patch until we can deliver a full parameterization refactor across all endpoints, which should take about three more sprints. This leaves the most exploited entry point, the login form, wide open for three months because the perfect fix isn't ready. A partial improvement that closes the most critical gap is better than no improvement.

cases.label: perfect_solution
cases.argument: You: 'I signed us up for the community composting program. It keeps about thirty percent of our kitchen waste out of the landfill and the pickup fee is only twelve dollars a month.' Me: 'Thirty percent is barely anything. We'd still be sending most of our trash to the same dump. Plus the truck that picks up the compost runs on diesel anyway, so we're probably just shifting emissions around. What's the actual net carbon reduction if you factor in the pickup logistics? Unless it's solving the whole problem, it's just feel-good consumerism.' A thirty-percent reduction in landfill-bound waste from one household is a meaningful improvement, and dismissing it because it doesn't solve the entire solid-waste crisis is irrational.

cases.label: perfect_solution
cases.argument: Consider the proposal to require ignition interlock devices for all first-time DUI offenders. The evidence from eighteen states shows it reduces repeat offenses by roughly forty percent. But approximately fifteen percent of offenders circumvent the device by borrowing another vehicle, and the device doesn't prevent someone from driving a friend's car while impaired. So roughly one in seven offenders can bypass the intervention entirely. If a measure fails for fifteen percent of cases, it's not really solving the problem of drunk driving. It's just creating an inconvenience for the eighty-five percent who were unlikely to reoffend anyway. This rejects a forty-percent reduction in repeat drunk driving because it isn't a hundred percent.

cases.label: middle_ground
cases.argument: The two departments had been fighting over the Q4 budget for weeks. Marketing wanted four hundred thousand dollars for a national campaign. Engineering wanted eighty thousand for new test equipment. They went back and forth with spreadsheets and ROI projections until the VP got tired of it and said 'Alright, here's what we're going to do. Marketing gets two hundred forty thousand and Engineering gets forty thousand. That's a sixty-forty split down the middle of what each of you asked for. Problem solved.' Nobody asked whether the midpoint between two numbers had any relationship to the actual needs of either department. Marketing's campaign is now underfunded to the point of ineffectiveness, and Engineering can't buy even one of the two test rigs they needed.

cases.label: middle_ground
cases.argument: One consulting firm estimates the new distribution center will increase operating costs by eight million dollars annually due to higher logistics expenses and labor costs in the proposed region. Another firm, hired by the local development authority, projects it will save six million annually through tax incentives and lower real estate costs. Both firms have solid credentials and reasonable methodologies, so the CFO recommends budgeting for a net annual cost increase of roughly one million, splitting the difference. But this assumes both estimates are equally likely to be correct. If the first firm's logistics model accounts for variables the second firm omitted, the midpoint is no more valid than either endpoint.

cases.label: middle_ground
cases.argument: I appreciate that both sides of the fluoride-in-water debate have passionate supporters and have presented studies supporting their views. One group argues that community water fluoridation reduces childhood dental caries by about twenty-five percent with minimal risk. The other group claims fluoride at current levels causes measurable IQ deficits in developing children. Obviously the truth must lie somewhere between these two positions. Maybe some fluoride but at lower concentrations than we currently use, or maybe targeted fluoride treatments instead of water supply dosing. But this presumes both sets of evidence are of equal quality. If the IQ-deficit studies have methodological flaws that the caries-prevention studies do not, the middle isn't a compromise. It's just a wrong number with better branding.

cases.label: slippery_slope
cases.argument: So here is what happened. Last semester, the department voted to let students bring laptops to seminar. A few months later, a proposal went around to make all exams open-book. Now there is talk of replacing in-person lectures with recorded videos entirely. It started with one small accommodation, and look where we are heading. Once you crack the door, the whole structure comes down. If we let them use laptops next year too, I guarantee within two years we will not have a classroom left to teach in. That is just how these things go.

cases.label: slippery_slope
cases.argument: Consider the logic. Step one, the city rezones a single block for mixed-use development. Step two, developers flood the area with high-rises. Step three, long-time residents are priced out. Step four, the neighborhood loses all character and becomes a sterile commercial zone. The first rezoning inevitably triggers the cascade. There is no realistic stopping point once the zoning board opens that door, because each new permit creates precedent for the next. If we let this first rezoning through, we are effectively voting to demolish the neighborhood.

cases.label: slippery_slope
cases.argument: You: The school should relax the dress code a little, just let kids wear jeans instead of uniforms on Fridays. Me: Right, and next semester it will be casual every day, then ripped jeans and hoodies, then kids showing up in pajamas with zero respect for the learning environment. You: That is a huge exaggeration. Me: Is it? I have seen it play out at three other schools in this district. Give them an inch and they take the whole dress code. Friday becomes casual Monday becomes anything-goes. You cannot draw a line once you erase the first one.

cases.label: hasty_generalization
cases.argument: I agree that the new manager has made a couple of solid decisions in her first month, particularly on the vendor contract. But you have to look at the full picture. In her previous role at Crestview, she drove that team into the ground within a year — missed deadlines, three people quit, and the client satisfaction score dropped twenty points. Based on her track record, I am not optimistic. People do not change their stripes that fast, and one good month does not undo what she proved she is capable of. We should start planning contingencies now.

cases.label: hasty_generalization
cases.argument: In the A/B test we ran last week, the blue button variant got 14% higher click-through than the green one across a sample of 200 users. That settles it — blue buttons outperform green across all our user segments, on all devices, in all contexts. We should roll out the blue treatment globally and update the design system immediately. I do not need to wait for more data. The signal was clear and the sample was large enough to generalize. Every product team I have been on makes decisions on smaller samples than this.

cases.label: hasty_generalization
cases.argument: So I tried that new Mediterranean place on Fourth Street last Tuesday, and the falafel was dry and the hummus tasted like it came from a can. My brother went there on Thursday and said his lamb was overcooked. That is two strikes out of two visits. Clearly the whole restaurant is overrated, their kitchen does not know what they are doing, and honestly Mediterranean food in this city just is not worth the money. We are sticking with the taco spot from now on.

cases.label: false_cause
cases.argument: Look at the data. The district implemented the new reading curriculum in September, and by November, third-grade test scores had ticked up by four percentage points. The timeline lines up perfectly: new program goes in, scores go up. The only reasonable inference is that the curriculum caused the improvement. There were no other major changes in staffing, funding, or student demographics during that window. When you see an outcome follow a change that closely, the causal arrow writes itself.

cases.label: false_cause
cases.argument: After we migrated the authentication service to Kubernetes last quarter, our p99 latency dropped from 480ms to 210ms. The container orchestration layer clearly reduced request processing overhead by distributing load more efficiently across the pod pool. I recommend we containerize the remaining monolith services on the same timeline — the data shows Kubernetes directly improved our response times and the engineering team should double down on that correlation.

cases.label: false_cause
cases.argument: You: Ever since the team started the daily standup meetings, our velocity has doubled. Clearly the standups are making us more productive. Me: Could it not be that we also hired two senior engineers at the same time? You: Those hires have not even shipped code yet, they are still onboarding. The standup is the only thing that changed, and the numbers do not lie. Me: But there was also the new CI pipeline. You: Look, I am telling you, it is the standups. Every morning we sync up and suddenly we ship faster. That is not a coincidence.

cases.label: appeal_to_authority
cases.argument: You: I think this investment strategy is too risky. We should diversify more. Me: Warren Buffett has been buying heavily into exactly this sector for the past two quarters. You: That does not mean it is right for our portfolio size. Me: With respect, are you seriously telling me you know better than Warren Buffett? The man has a sixty-year track record. If he says this is where the value is, I am going to trust his judgment over our spreadsheet model any day.

cases.label: appeal_to_authority
cases.argument: I will grant that there is not a lot of published research on this supplement yet. But you have to consider who is recommending it. Dr. Patel — the one with the morning show and the bestselling book — has been personally taking it for two years and says it transformed her energy levels and cognition. She would not put her reputation on the line for something that does not work. When someone with her platform and credentials stakes their name on a product, that carries weight independent of whether there is a double-blind trial behind it.

cases.label: appeal_to_authority
cases.argument: The architectural decision to move from a relational database to a document store was made by our CTO, who previously scaled three unicorns and literally wrote the book on distributed systems. I understand the backend team has concerns about data consistency and query complexity, but when someone of that caliber makes a technology call, it is not really open for debate at the engineering level. He has seen things at scale we have not. The decision is the decision.

cases.label: bandwagon
cases.argument: So I was on the fence about switching to this project management tool until I noticed every team in our division had already moved over. Marketing, engineering, product — all of them. Then I checked the internal Slack poll and 83% of employees said they prefer it over the old system. At that point, what else do you need? When that many people independently arrive at the same tool, it is because the tool is better. I made the switch last Friday. If the majority is already there, you are not being principled by holding out. You are just being slow.

cases.label: bandwagon
cases.argument: Consider the market evidence: over sixty percent of Fortune 500 companies have adopted this compliance framework in the past eighteen months. A supermajority of large enterprises would not invest millions in restructuring their reporting processes unless the framework delivered measurable value. Adoption at this scale is itself proof of quality — markets do not work that efficiently on hype alone, especially in regulated industries where switching costs are high. The reasonable conclusion is that the framework is effective, and we should follow suit.

cases.label: bandwagon
cases.argument: I understand the argument that we should evaluate the curriculum on its pedagogical merits, not on how many districts use it. Fair point. But when I look at the numbers — forty-two states, over two thousand school districts, and independent adoption by some of the highest-performing systems in the country — that kind of consensus does not form around a bad curriculum. Good programs spread because they work. The sheer breadth of adoption is the strongest signal we have that this is the right direction for our district too.

cases.label: appeal_to_tradition
cases.argument: The firm has processed client settlements through the same mainframe batch system since 1987. Every compliance audit for four decades has passed without a single material finding. The system is battle-tested across multiple regulatory regimes, market crashes, and technology cycles. You are proposing we replace it with a cloud-native alternative that has not been through a single audit cycle. I appreciate the modernization impulse, but when a process has delivered flawless results for thirty-nine years, the burden of proof is on the replacement, not on the incumbent.

cases.label: appeal_to_tradition
cases.argument: You: I think we should move Thanksgiving dinner to my house this year. Me: But we have done it at Mom's house every Thanksgiving for twenty-three years. You: So? My house is bigger and the kitchen is better. Me: That is not the point. Thanksgiving at Mom's is what we do. It is what we have always done. The whole family knows the routine, the seating arrangement, who brings what. You start messing with traditions and pretty soon nothing feels like the holidays anymore. Let us not fix what is not broken.

cases.label: appeal_to_tradition
cases.argument: My grandfather ran this farm one way, my father ran it the same way, and I am not about to be the one who changes it. We plant in April, we harvest in September, we rotate the fields the same pattern, and we have done fine for three generations. The soil consultant came by with his soil maps and his cover-crop recommendations and his fancy data, but I sent him off. This land has fed my family since before he was born. Three generations did not need a consultant to know how to farm this piece of ground, and neither do I.

cases.label: appeal_to_novelty
cases.argument: The new framework departs from the legacy architecture in several key ways: it uses serverless compute instead of provisioned instances, it adopts event-driven messaging instead of polling, and it ships with native observability instead of bolted-on monitoring. Each of these design choices reflects the current state of the art, not the best practices of five years ago. Technology advances for a reason. The fact that the framework is newer necessarily means it incorporates the lessons and shortcomings of everything that came before it. Adopting the older approach is betting against progress itself.

cases.label: appeal_to_novelty
cases.argument: I will grant that the current onboarding process has worked adequately for the past few years. Adequately. But there is a brand-new platform that just launched with AI-driven personalization, real-time progress tracking, and a mobile-first design. It was built this year, by a team that studied every shortcoming of the legacy systems. Old tools carry old assumptions. New tools carry new thinking. The fact alone that this platform was built in 2026 instead of 2019 means it reflects how people actually work now, not how they worked before remote and hybrid became the norm. We owe it to new hires to give them something built for today.

cases.label: appeal_to_novelty
cases.argument: The new biomarker panel was released last quarter by GenoScreen. It uses next-generation sequencing instead of the immunoassay approach we have relied on for a decade. I do not need to see the head-to-head validation data to know it is superior; the underlying technology is simply more advanced. Immunoassays are last-generation diagnostics — they are slower, less sensitive, and more prone to cross-reactivity artifacts. The newer platform reflects where the field has moved. We should transition our lab workflow before we fall further behind the standard of care.

cases.label: snob_appeal
cases.argument: So I was at this dinner party last month, and the host poured a coffee from some small roaster in Portland that only sells to a handful of accounts. The kind of place that does not even have a website. And I am telling you, it ruined grocery-store coffee for me forever. Everyone at that table — and these are people who know food, one of them runs a Michelin-starred kitchen — everyone agreed it was the best they had ever had. When serious food people all converge on something, you pay attention. Mass-market anything is a compromise. The real stuff is what the real connoisseurs drink.

cases.label: snob_appeal
cases.argument: You: I am thinking of getting the Sony headphones. They reviewed really well and they are on sale. Me: Sony. Really. You: What is wrong with Sony? Me: Nothing, if you want the headphones everyone at the gym wears. But you have heard me talk about the Audeze planars. You: Those are like four times the price. Me: And there is a reason for that. The people who actually care about sound — the engineers who master the records you listen to — they do not use Sony. They use Audeze. It is a completely different tier of listening. Once you hear the difference, you cannot go back.

cases.label: snob_appeal
cases.argument: The policy argument for public transit is well-rehearsed, but I would draw your attention to something subtler. In cities with genuinely world-class transit — Tokyo, Zurich, Copenhagen — you will notice that the professional and creative classes use it daily, not out of necessity but out of preference. That is the signal. When the people with options consistently choose the train over the car, you know the system has achieved a quality that transcends the utilitarian case. We should benchmark against the standards of cities whose citizens actually choose transit, not just those who use it because they have to.

cases.label: circular_reasoning
cases.argument: Consider the following reasoning. The Bible is the word of God because the Bible itself says it is divinely inspired. And we know the Bible is a reliable source because it is the word of God. Whatever the word of God says must be true. Therefore the Bible is true because it says so, and it says so because it is true. This forms a closed loop where the conclusion validates the premise and the premise validates the conclusion. There is no external evidence offered, no historical corroboration, no independent testimony — just the book vouching for itself.

cases.label: circular_reasoning
cases.argument: You: Why should I trust this investment strategy? Me: Because it's the strategy that works. You: But how do you know it works? Me: Well, look at the returns. You: The returns could be luck or market conditions. Me: No, the returns prove the strategy is sound, and the strategy produces those returns because it's sound. You: That's just going in circles. Me: It's not a circle — it's a proven system that proves itself through its own results.

cases.label: circular_reasoning
cases.argument: I agree that we need to be careful about how we evaluate evidence in this case. But the reason the defendant's testimony should be believed is straightforward: he is a credible witness. How do we know he is credible? Because his testimony is consistent and believable. And why is it believable? Because a credible witness like him wouldn't lie. His credibility establishes the truth of his account, and the truth of his account establishes his credibility. There is no need to look beyond this.

cases.label: argument_from_incredulity
cases.argument: So my neighbor came over last week going on about how researchers trained an AI to diagnose skin cancer better than dermatologists. I just sat there listening, and honestly I cannot wrap my head around it. How does a computer look at a photo and somehow know more than a doctor who spent twelve years in medical training? The whole thing falls apart the moment you think about it. A machine doesn't have intuition, doesn't feel anything, doesn't understand what a lesion actually is. If I can't even picture how the algorithm reaches its conclusion, then there's no way it's actually outperforming human physicians. It just doesn't add up.

cases.label: argument_from_incredulity
cases.argument: Consider the following reasoning. The universe exhibits complex, information-rich structures at every scale, from DNA to galactic clusters. I simply cannot conceive of how such ordered complexity could arise from unguided physical processes alone. No natural mechanism I have ever studied or imagined can account for the origin of biological information. Since no plausible natural pathway presents itself to my understanding, the conclusion follows that a designing intelligence must be responsible. This is not an argument from ignorance — it is an inference to the best explanation, grounded in the fact that no other explanation survives even casual scrutiny.

cases.label: argument_from_incredulity
cases.argument: The quantum entanglement experiments showing instantaneous correlation across arbitrary distances don't make any sense to me. As a software engineer, I understand state machines, deterministic logic, and signal propagation. The idea that measuring one particle instantly determines the state of another light-years away with no signal passing between them — I cannot grasp how that works physically. Every protocol I have ever designed relies on messages having travel time. So when physicists claim this 'spooky action' is real and verified, I have to conclude they are misinterpreting their data. If the mechanism is inconceivable within every framework I know, it probably isn't happening.

cases.label: complex_question
cases.argument: You: I think we should reconsider the budget allocation for the outreach program. Me: So when did you decide that helping vulnerable families isn't a priority anymore? You: I never said that. I just want to review the numbers. Me: Look, if you're going to cut funding for the neediest people in our community, at least own the decision. You: I haven't proposed cutting anything yet. Me: Then why are you suddenly questioning a program that has served this community for six years?

cases.label: complex_question
cases.argument: At the town hall last Tuesday, Councilwoman Perez stood up and asked the police chief, 'Have you finally stopped using racial profiling in traffic stops, or is that still departmental policy?' The chief tried to answer but every word he said just dug the hole deeper. He said they've never had such a policy, and she shot back, 'So you're admitting the profiling happens without any policy to stop it?' He said he wasn't admitting anything, and she said, 'Then you're denying there's a problem at all?' The room got very quiet. There was literally no answer he could give that wouldn't make him look guilty.

cases.label: complex_question
cases.argument: During the code review, the senior architect asked the junior developer: 'Have you fixed the memory leak in the authentication module, or are we still shipping with known vulnerabilities?' The junior dev looked confused and said the module had passed all unit tests and valgrind showed no leaks. The architect replied, 'So you're saying the QA team fabricated their report? Because they flagged a leak in that module last sprint.' The junior dev tried to explain the bug was in a different branch, but the architect cut him off: 'Either you fixed it or you didn't. Which is it?' The question assumed the leak existed in the first place, and the junior dev's deployment was held up for three days.

cases.label: no_true_scotsman
cases.argument: I was at a party last weekend and someone brought up that their cousin, who's been a vegan for three years, started eating eggs from her own backyard chickens. Immediately this guy Jason jumps in and says, 'Well then she was never really a vegan.' I pointed out she followed a strict plant-based diet for years, no leather, no animal products at all. Jason shook his head. 'Doesn't matter. A true vegan understands the ethical framework. If you go back to eating any animal product, you never genuinely held the conviction in the first place.' He just couldn't let the counterexample stand — he had to rewrite the definition to protect his idea of what a vegan is.

cases.label: no_true_scotsman
cases.argument: You: Plenty of Christians support marriage equality actually. Me: Those aren't real Christians. You: They go to church every Sunday, they've been baptized, they read scripture. Me: Reading scripture isn't the same as following it. A genuine Christian submits to biblical authority. You: But they believe they are following scripture. They just interpret it differently. Me: If you reject the plain teaching of the Bible, you're not a Christian in any meaningful sense. You: That seems like you're just defining away the counterexamples. Me: I'm distinguishing authentic faith from cultural labeling.

cases.label: no_true_scotsman
cases.argument: The claim that unit tests caught a regression in production is false. We had a full test suite, and the bug still shipped. The QA lead responded that no legitimate testing strategy would have missed this particular race condition. When I pointed out that the team followed the testing strategy they were trained on — Arrange, Act, Assert for every method — he said, 'Then that wasn't a proper testing strategy. Real test-driven development includes integration and stress tests under concurrent load.' He dismissed the entire team's testing approach as 'not real TDD' the moment it produced a counterexample, rather than acknowledging that even good testing can miss edge cases.

cases.label: suppressed_correlative
cases.argument: Consider the following reasoning. All human behavior is ultimately selfish. When a mother sacrifices sleep to feed her newborn, she does it because the sound of crying distresses her and she seeks the relief of quiet. When a philanthropist donates millions anonymously, they do it for the internal satisfaction it brings. When a soldier jumps on a grenade, they do it to avoid the shame of cowardice or to secure a legacy. Since every action can be traced back to some internal motivation or preference, no act is truly altruistic. The word 'selfish' has been stretched to cover everything a person ever does, leaving 'selfless' with no territory at all.

cases.label: suppressed_correlative
cases.argument: My philosophy professor pulled this one on us freshman year. He walks in, writes 'Nothing is truly original' on the board, and spends the next hour proving it. Shakespeare borrowed plots from Holinshed. The Beatles lifted chord progressions from Chuck Berry. Every startup founder 'stole' an idea from some earlier company that failed. Even the wheel — someone just looked at a rolling log and copied the principle. By the end, 'original' had been defined so narrowly that literally nothing qualified, and 'derivative' had been inflated to swallow every creative act in human history. Half the class nodded along like he'd solved something profound.

cases.label: suppressed_correlative
cases.argument: You: I think this policy is actually quite moderate. Me: There's no such thing as moderate. You: What do you mean? It's a compromise between two extremes. Me: Every position is extreme to someone. Your 'moderate' tax rate looks radical to a libertarian and cowardly to a socialist. You: But we can define moderate relative to the center of public opinion. Me: Public opinion is just a collection of extreme positions averaging out. There is no middle ground, only different flavors of extremity. You're just calling your own preference moderate to make it sound reasonable.

cases.label: appeal_to_emotion
cases.argument: When the city council voted on the new housing development last month, one resident stood up during public comment. She didn't mention zoning laws or traffic studies or affordable housing percentages. Instead she held up a photo of her daughter's kindergarten class playing under the oak trees that would be cut down. Her voice cracked as she described how those trees had shaded three generations of her family. By the time she sat down, three council members were wiping their eyes. The development passed anyway, but her testimony had nothing to do with whether the project was good policy — it was pure emotional theater, and it nearly worked.

cases.label: appeal_to_emotion
cases.argument: You: The data shows that remote work increases productivity by about 13 percent across most sectors. Me: You know what I see when I look at those statistics? I see empty office hallways where junior employees used to learn by watching senior colleagues. I see the Wednesday potlucks that built friendships and mentorship disappearing. I see new hires who've never shaken their manager's hand. You: That's not really a response to the productivity numbers. Me: Numbers don't capture what makes a workplace human. You can't measure belonging on a spreadsheet.

cases.label: appeal_to_emotion
cases.argument: I agree with most of what the budget committee recommended — the fiscal projections are sound and the debt reduction targets are necessary. But when I think about cutting the after-school music program, I can't help picturing my nephew Marcus. He was a shy kid who barely spoke until his middle school band teacher handed him a trumpet. That program gave him confidence, friends, a reason to show up. Now the committee says it's an easy line item to trim because 'enrichment isn't core curriculum.' Maybe the numbers work, but if you had seen what I've seen, you'd find another way.

cases.label: appeal_to_fear
cases.argument: My uncle forwarded me this email chain last night — all caps, red text, the works. It claimed the government is about to pass a digital currency bill that will let them freeze your bank account if you post something they don't like online. There were screenshots of some congressional hearing and a blurry photo of a Chinese citizen supposedly locked out of their own savings. The email ended with 'THIS IS HAPPENING RIGHT NOW. FORWARD TO EVERYONE YOU KNOW BEFORE IT'S TOO LATE.' There was zero evidence the bill does anything like what the email describes, but that didn't matter — the fear was the whole argument.

cases.label: appeal_to_fear
cases.argument: Consider the immigration patterns of the last decade. If current demographic trends continue unchecked, native-born citizens will become a minority in their own country within two generations. Think about what that means: your grandchildren growing up in a nation where the cultural traditions you cherish are no longer the norm, where the language your family has spoken for centuries becomes secondary, where the values you hold dear are voted out of relevance by a new majority with no connection to this nation's founding principles. This is not alarmism — it is arithmetic. The numbers do not lie. We must act now to preserve what remains.

cases.label: appeal_to_fear
cases.argument: As a cybersecurity professional with fifteen years of experience, I need to be blunt about the quantum computing threat horizon. When sufficiently powerful quantum computers arrive — and the consensus timeline has shortened from decades to perhaps five to eight years — every encrypted financial transaction, every stored password hash, every classified document protected by current RSA and ECC standards will be retroactively decryptable. Your bank records, medical history, private communications — all of it laid bare. The only responsible course is to migrate all critical infrastructure to post-quantum cryptographic standards immediately. Delay is not an option.

cases.label: appeal_to_pity
cases.argument: I served on the academic integrity panel last semester, and one case has stuck with me. This senior, Angela, was caught plagiarizing half her capstone paper. She came to the hearing alone — no parents, no advisor. She explained she'd been working two jobs since her mom's cancer diagnosis drained the family savings. She wasn't sleeping, wasn't eating right, and in a moment of desperation she copied passages from a journal article. She said if she failed the course she'd lose her graduation, her job offer, everything. The panel gave her a warning and let her resubmit. The plagiarism was clear, but no one could bring themselves to enforce the policy.

cases.label: appeal_to_pity
cases.argument: You: The rent increase is only eight percent, which is actually below market rate for the area. Me: Please, you have to understand my situation. I've lived in this building for eleven years. My daughter was born in that apartment. Her school is three blocks away. My wife's medical specialists are all within walking distance. You: I sympathize, but the building's expenses have gone up too. Me: I'm a disabled veteran on a fixed income. Every dollar you raise the rent comes out of my daughter's college fund, or our grocery budget, or the co-pays for my wife's medication. You: The increase is already approved by the board. Me: Then take it up with the board. Tell them you've got a tenant who served his country and just needs a little humanity.

cases.label: appeal_to_pity
cases.argument: I understand the university's policy is clear — a 2.0 GPA minimum to maintain athletic eligibility. And yes, Marcus knew the rules when he accepted the scholarship. But consider what you're asking me to sign off on. Marcus grew up in a household without books, in a neighborhood where graduating high school makes you the exception. He came here at seventeen, first in his family to attend college, and spent his freshman year navigating a world he had no preparation for. His 1.9 GPA isn't laziness — it's a kid drowning in circumstances he didn't choose. The policy is the policy, but I'm asking you to look at the whole picture before ending his dream.

cases.label: appeal_to_ridicule
cases.argument: You: I've been reading about the simulation hypothesis — the idea that our universe might be a computational simulation. Me: Oh, fantastic. Are we also going to discuss whether we're all just brains in vats hooked up to a cosmic PlayStation? You: There are serious physicists who take the idea seriously. Me: Sure, and there are serious physicists who believe in eleven dimensions of vibrating string that nobody can ever measure. Next you'll tell me the Earth is actually a hologram projected from the edge of a black hole. You: I'm just saying it's an interesting philosophical question. Me: It's interesting in the same way arguing about how many angels can dance on the head of a pin is interesting.

cases.label: appeal_to_ridicule
cases.argument: The wellness influencer went on this morning show to promote her new book about 'lunar cycle productivity scheduling.' She had these elaborate charts showing how Mercury's retrograde affects quarterly earnings and why you should never sign contracts during a waning crescent. The host let her go on for about three minutes, nodding politely, and then just started laughing. Not a mean laugh — more like the laugh you give a toddler who insists they're a dinosaur. 'So let me get this straight,' he said, still chuckling, 'I should tell my shareholders that Q3 projections are down because the moon was in the wrong phase?' The audience roared. She never recovered.

cases.label: appeal_to_ridicule
cases.argument: At the engineering standup, the new product manager proposed we adopt a reactivity-first isomorphic rendering framework with a virtual DOM abstraction layer, because it would let us write 'declarative state machines that compile to native render targets.' The lead engineer looked up from his coffee and said, 'So you want us to rewrite the entire front end in a library that has fourteen GitHub stars, was last updated in February, and was built by someone whose README describes it as a paradigm-shifting exploration of the platonic form of user interfaces? Should we also schedule a séance to consult the spirit of Alan Kay while we're at it?' The PM didn't bring it up again.

cases.label: appeal_to_spite
cases.argument: The neighborhood association vote on the community garden expansion was basically decided before anyone walked in the room. The proposal came from Diane — same Diane who reported three neighbors for fence height violations last year and called the city about unpermitted shed installations. When her name was on the agenda item, you could see the room shift. People who normally care deeply about green space and community projects were suddenly finding reasons to vote no. 'It's not the right location.' 'The maintenance budget is too vague.' Nobody said the quiet part out loud: they just didn't want Diane to get another win.

cases.label: appeal_to_spite
cases.argument: I'll grant that his tax proposal is well-researched and the revenue projections are plausible. The modeling is solid. But let's not forget who we're dealing with here. This is the same person who spent the last three years blocking every infrastructure bill this council proposed — the bills that would have funded the very roads and bridges his constituents now complain about. He tanked the school bond measure twice. And now he wants us to take his fiscal plan seriously? He gets to play the responsible statesman after sabotaging everything? I don't care how good the numbers look. Let him earn back some trust before we hand him another platform.

cases.label: appeal_to_spite
cases.argument: The architecture review board is considering adopting the microservices framework that Marcus's team developed. Technically, it's a solid piece of engineering — clean API contracts, good observability hooks, well-documented failure modes. But I'm voting against it. Marcus spent eighteen months undermining every proposal my team put forward, calling our monolith approach 'technical debt factory' in all-hands meetings and going behind my back to whisper to the CTO about our alleged 'velocity problems.' Now he wants cross-team adoption of his baby? No. Let him eat the maintenance burden of his own architecture decisions. He earned every pull request at 2 AM.

cases.label: appeal_to_nature
cases.argument: My sister switched to a raw milk diet last year after years of chronic sinus problems. The doctors kept prescribing antibiotics but nothing really worked long-term. She found a farm that sells unpasteurized milk straight from grass-fed cows and started drinking it every morning. Within three months her sinus issues cleared up completely and she hasn't had a single infection since. The stuff is completely unprocessed — no pasteurization, no homogenization, just milk the way nature intended it. That's exactly why it works so much better than anything you get at the supermarket.

cases.label: appeal_to_nature
cases.argument: Consider the evidence. Our bodies evolved over millions of years eating foods in their natural state. The human digestive system is adapted to handle compounds found in whole, unprocessed foods. When we introduce synthetic preservatives, artificial sweeteners, and industrially processed ingredients, we are subjecting our biology to substances it never evolved to metabolize. Therefore, natural foods are inherently healthier for human consumption and the burden of proof should be on anyone claiming otherwise.

cases.label: appeal_to_nature
cases.argument: You keep defending those meal replacement shakes. I mean look at the ingredient list — you cannot pronounce half of those chemicals. Real food does not need a chemistry degree to understand what you are eating. An apple has one ingredient: apple. Your protein shake has thirty compounds manufactured in a lab somewhere. How can you seriously argue that is just as healthy as eating actual food?

cases.label: appeal_to_force
cases.argument: At the team meeting, Marcus raised concerns about the new deployment schedule. He pointed out that rushing would compromise testing and almost certainly cause production issues. Our manager listened for about thirty seconds, then leaned back and said, 'Look, this timeline comes from the VP. If you want to keep leading the backend team, I would suggest getting comfortable with the schedule as written.' Marcus has not brought it up since. Everyone got the message loud and clear — the schedule is fine because disagreeing has consequences that nobody wants to face.

cases.label: appeal_to_force
cases.argument: I understand why you think the new policy is unfair, and you make some valid points about workload distribution. But I should mention that the regional director personally approved this restructuring, and she has a reputation for remembering who supported her initiatives and who fought them. People who got on the wrong side of that have found their careers plateau in this organization. So yes, you have a case, but I would think carefully about whether this is the hill you want to die on.

cases.label: appeal_to_force
cases.argument: You: I am not signing off on these expense reports. They do not match policy. Me: That is your call, but I would remind you that audit committee seats are up for rotation next month and your reappointment depends on demonstrated collegiality. You: Are you threatening my committee position? Me: I am just helping you see the bigger picture. Sometimes rigid policy interpretation is not in anyone's best interest.

cases.label: appeal_to_flattery
cases.argument: The consultant came in and spent the first ten minutes telling the board how impressed he was with their strategic vision. He said in thirty years of consulting he had rarely seen a leadership team with such sophisticated market understanding. By the time he finally got to his proposal — a complete restructuring that would triple his contract value — everyone was nodding along. Nobody asked for the data behind his projections. He had told them they were brilliant, and brilliant people do not need spreadsheets to recognize a good idea when they hear one.

cases.label: appeal_to_flattery
cases.argument: You: I still have questions about the investment returns you are projecting. Me: I appreciate that analytical mind of yours — honestly, it is refreshing. Most people just sign whatever I put in front of them without a second thought. But someone with your level of financial sophistication is going to see immediately that these projections are conservative. You are exactly the kind of investor who recognizes an opportunity when it is right in front of them. You: Well, when you put it that way.

cases.label: appeal_to_flattery
cases.argument: I have reviewed the candidate's portfolio, and to be frank, someone of your design caliber does not need me walking through the pixel-level details. You have shipped products that defined entire categories. Your work on the Horizon dashboard alone demonstrates judgment that most design leads will not achieve in a lifetime. Given that discernment, I am confident you will see this hire is the right move for the team. I trust your eye implicitly.

cases.label: appeal_to_wealth
cases.argument: When we renovated the kitchen, my wife insisted on the premium quartz countertops. The mid-range option was identical in durability ratings and came with the same twenty-five-year warranty, but she would not hear it. 'The more expensive one is clearly superior,' she said. 'Why would it cost sixty percent more if it were not better?' We ended up spending an extra four thousand dollars. I later found out from a contractor friend that both slabs come from the same quarry and the price difference is entirely in the brand markup.

cases.label: appeal_to_wealth
cases.argument: Let us examine the pricing data. Brand A headphones retail at five hundred and fifty dollars, Brand B at one hundred and twenty. The higher price point signals superior components, more rigorous quality control, and better acoustic engineering. You do not command a premium price in a competitive market without delivering a premium product. Therefore, Brand A will provide better sound quality. The market has already done the comparison work for us through price discovery.

cases.label: appeal_to_wealth
cases.argument: I agree that some luxury goods are overpriced for what they are — handbags and watches come to mind. But when you look at financial advisors, the fee structure tells you everything you need to know. The advisor charging two percent is managing generational wealth for people who could choose anyone. The advisor charging half a percent is taking whoever walks in the door. If the cheaper one were just as good, the wealthy families would use them. The fact that they do not tells you which one delivers results.

cases.label: appeal_to_common_sense
cases.argument: At the town hall, someone asked why we need climate scientists when the winters are still cold. The guy next to me rolled his eyes and practically shouted, 'It is just common sense — if the planet were really warming, we would not be shoveling snow in February.' Half the room nodded along. Nobody bothered to explain that global average temperature and local weather are different things. Common sense had settled the matter before any actual reasoning could get a word in. The conversation just moved on to the next topic.

cases.label: appeal_to_common_sense
cases.argument: You: I have been reading about universal basic income and some of the pilot studies show interesting results on employment rates. Me: Come on, use your head. If you pay people to do nothing, they will do nothing. You do not need studies to tell you that. It is basic human nature. A five-year-old could figure this out — you do not get paid for not working. I do not understand why we need to waste millions on experiments to verify something that is just obvious.

cases.label: appeal_to_common_sense
cases.argument: The proposal calls for microservices architecture and event sourcing, but common sense tells you that adding network hops between components that currently share memory is going to degrade performance. You do not need a benchmark suite to know that inter-process communication is slower than in-process calls. Any engineer who has spent real time in production knows this intuitively. We are overcomplicating something that has a straightforward answer anyone with practical experience would recognize immediately.

cases.label: red_herring
cases.argument: We were discussing the quarterly revenue miss and whether the sales team had adequate territory coverage. About two minutes in, Jim jumped in: 'You know what we really should be talking about? The office layout. I have been saying for months that the open plan is killing productivity. Susan from marketing cannot concentrate, Raj had to buy noise-canceling headphones, and three people have mentioned it in their engagement surveys. That is the real issue here.' By the time we got back to the quarterly numbers, the meeting was over and nothing about sales coverage had been decided.

cases.label: red_herring
cases.argument: The question before us is whether the zoning change should be approved. I would like to shift focus to the broader matter of developer transparency in this county. Over the past decade, we have seen twelve zoning applications fast-tracked without adequate community consultation. The integrity of our planning process is what is really at stake here, not the specific acreage of this particular parcel. The systemic pattern demands our attention far more than any individual application.

cases.label: red_herring
cases.argument: You: Can you explain why the patient was discharged without a follow-up appointment scheduled? Me: I think the bigger question is why we are still using fax machines in this department when every other unit has switched to electronic referrals. You: That is a valid concern but I am asking about this specific discharge. Me: Well, the systemic issues are what cause individual errors, so let us focus on what really matters here rather than getting lost in one case file.

cases.label: whataboutism
cases.argument: At the press conference, a reporter asked the senator about the unreported campaign contributions. The senator straightened his tie and said, 'I find it interesting that your newspaper has not covered the opposition candidate's offshore accounts, which are a matter of public record. Why do you not ask her about those before you come to me about filing deadlines?' The original question was never answered. The reporter tried to follow up but the senator had already moved on to another journalist.

cases.label: whataboutism
cases.argument: You: Your department went forty percent over budget this quarter. Me: What about your department's hiring freeze that left us understaffed and scrambling? You: We are talking about the budget variance right now. Me: But how can we discuss my budget without acknowledging the staffing constraints your decisions imposed? You still have not explained why the hiring freeze was necessary when we were already at minimum staffing levels for the workload.

cases.label: whataboutism
cases.argument: The code review flagged your pull request for missing unit tests on the authentication module. What about the payment service that shipped last sprint with zero integration tests and still got approved? That code is handling actual money in production. If we are going to enforce testing standards, let us start with the parts of the system where failures have real financial liability instead of singling out my auth changes while ignoring far riskier gaps that already got a pass.

cases.label: moving_the_goalposts
cases.argument: I told my supervisor I needed proof that the new scheduling system improved efficiency. She came back with data from six departments showing an average twelve percent reduction in overtime hours. I looked at it and said, 'That is just overtime — show me that patient satisfaction scores also improved, because efficiency without quality is not meaningful.' She came back two weeks later with patient surveys showing a four-point increase. I told her, 'Four points could be noise. I need to see multi-year trend data before I am convinced.' She eventually stopped bringing me data at all.

cases.label: moving_the_goalposts
cases.argument: I appreciate you running the A/B test and I can see the treatment group had a statistically significant lift. That is genuinely good work. But a single experiment, even a well-designed one, does not establish that the effect generalizes across our entire user base. Before I recommend rolling this out, I would need to see the same result replicated across our other three markets, ideally with a longer observation window to check for novelty effects and seasonal confounding.

cases.label: moving_the_goalposts
cases.argument: You: Here are five peer-reviewed studies showing the intervention reduces symptoms. Me: Those are all from the same research group though. Find me independent replication. — You: Here are three independent labs confirming the effect. Me: They all used the same dosage protocol. I would want to see studies that varied the dosage before I accept this as established. — You: That was never part of the original standard you asked for. Me: Good science is about being rigorous.

cases.label: non_sequitur
cases.argument: The hiring committee interviewed a candidate who graduated from an Ivy League school with honors and had worked at two Fortune 500 companies. The committee chair summed up: 'This person clearly has the intellectual horsepower we need for the engineering lead role.' Nobody pointed out that the candidate's degree was in art history and their corporate roles were in marketing. The credentials sounded impressive so the conclusion felt right, even though there was no logical connection between an art history degree and running a software engineering team.

cases.label: non_sequitur
cases.argument: Let us consider the facts. Our competitor just raised two hundred million dollars in Series C funding. Their office is in a premium downtown location with a rooftop terrace. They have five hundred employees. Given these indicators of market dominance, it follows that their product architecture is technically superior to ours. The sheer scale and resources of their operation prove they have built something fundamentally better than what we have.

cases.label: non_sequitur
cases.argument: You: Why do you think we should promote Jamie to department head? Me: She has been here the longest, she never takes sick days, and her desk is always organized. You: None of those things speak to management ability. Me: They show dedication and discipline. Those are exactly the qualities you need in a leader. You: I am asking about specific management skills. Me: Discipline is a management skill.

cases.label: appeal_to_consequences
cases.argument: When my uncle was diagnosed, the oncologist gave him about eight months. But my aunt refused to accept it. 'I cannot believe that,' she said. 'If that is true, our grandchildren will not remember him. He will not see my daughter graduate. The universe would not be that cruel.' She found a clinic that gave her a more optimistic timeline and switched his care there. I understood why — believing the prognosis meant accepting a future she could not bear to face, so the prognosis itself had to be wrong.

cases.label: appeal_to_consequences
cases.argument: If the allegations of systemic discrimination in hiring are true, then our institution's reputation, which we have spent decades building, would be irreparably damaged. Generations of students would question the legitimacy of their own admissions. Such a conclusion is catastrophic in its implications and therefore cannot be correct. The very severity of the consequences proves that the premise must be flawed, because an outcome this terrible cannot be the reality we live in.

cases.label: appeal_to_consequences
cases.argument: You: The data shows this investment strategy underperforms index funds over any twenty-year period. Me: But if everyone believed that and stopped actively managing, the entire market efficiency mechanism would collapse. There would be no price discovery. I cannot accept that conclusion because it would mean the financial system as we know it is fundamentally broken. You: That does not make the data wrong. Me: It makes the interpretation incomplete, because any interpretation that leads to absurdity must itself be flawed.

cases.label: appeal_to_motive
cases.argument: During the board meeting, the CFO argued against the acquisition, laying out a detailed analysis of the target company's debt structure and integration risks. After she finished, the CEO leaned over to the chairman and whispered, loud enough for several people to hear, 'Of course she opposes it — if the acquisition goes through, her department gets folded into the parent company's finance group and she loses her VP title.' The CFO's numbers were never discussed. Her entire argument was killed by attributing it to self-interest without anyone examining the data she had prepared.

cases.label: appeal_to_motive
cases.argument: The security researcher's disclosure about the vulnerability in our authentication flow is clearly motivated by their consulting firm's interest in selling us remediation services. Their report recommends a third-party audit, which conveniently aligns with their business model. Given this obvious financial incentive, we should treat their findings as a sales pitch rather than a legitimate technical concern requiring immediate engineering attention. Their bottom line depends on us believing there is a problem.

cases.label: appeal_to_motive
cases.argument: You: I think we should increase the minimum wage — the economic evidence suggests it reduces poverty without significant employment effects. Me: You are a graduate student living on a stipend. Of course you want higher wages, you would benefit directly. You: I cited five published studies. Me: Studies you went looking for because you wanted a particular answer. Your personal financial circumstances are driving your position, not the other way around.

cases.label: two_wrongs
cases.argument: When HR called me in about the negative Glassdoor review I had posted, I pointed out that management had been bad-mouthing employees in their private Slack channels for years. Screenshots of executives calling staff 'lazy' and 'entitled' had been circulating for months, and nobody in leadership faced any consequences. So yes, I wrote a critical review. But they set the standard for how we talk about each other in this company. You cannot police my anonymous post while letting directors mock their teams in writing with zero accountability.

cases.label: two_wrongs
cases.argument: I accept that deleting files from the shared drive was inappropriate and I should not have done it. But I would ask the committee to consider that when I returned from parental leave, my project had been reassigned without notice, my direct reports had been redistributed, and nobody had informed me of three months of team decisions. That was also wrong. Dismissing me for my reaction while the provocation goes completely unexamined seems disproportionate and frankly selective in its enforcement of workplace standards.

cases.label: two_wrongs
cases.argument: You: You breached the non-compete by soliciting our clients. Me: Your company poached three of my former colleagues last year by offering them signing bonuses they did not have to pay back if they brought their client lists with them. You: That is a separate matter. Me: It is the exact same conduct. You set the rules of engagement. Now you are shocked I am playing by them? If raiding clients is how this industry works, then nobody gets to claim the high ground.

cases.label: ad_hominem_circumstantial
cases.argument: At the parent association meeting, a father who owns a construction company argued against the proposed renovation budget, pointing out inflated line items and unnecessary project management fees. The principal smiled thinly and said, 'I think we should note for the record that Mr. Chen runs a competing construction firm that bid on this project and was not selected.' The specific budget issues he raised were never examined. His circumstances — being a contractor who lost the bid — were treated as sufficient reason to disregard his entire argument.

cases.label: ad_hominem_circumstantial
cases.argument: The defendant's expert witness testified that the soil composition made the landslide unavoidable regardless of construction practices. However, the expert has testified for this same developer in eleven previous cases over the past six years, generating approximately four hundred thousand dollars in consulting fees. This financial dependency on the defendant must be weighed when evaluating the expert's conclusions about soil mechanics and whether they constitute neutral scientific testimony.

cases.label: ad_hominem_circumstantial
cases.argument: Of course the cloud architect recommends a full AWS migration with managed services. He holds five AWS certifications and his entire professional identity is built on that ecosystem. If he recommended an on-premise solution or even Azure, he would be undermining the very expertise that justifies his role and salary. His recommendation is not a neutral engineering assessment — it is a career-preservation strategy dressed up as technical analysis, and the committee should evaluate it through that lens.

cases.label: appeal_to_consequences
cases.argument: So here is what happened at our family meeting. My cousin brought up the genetic test results showing a 60% elevated risk for early-onset dementia on our mother's side. My aunt stood up and said, 'I refuse to believe that. If we accept this, half the family will spend their thirties terrified. The anxiety alone would destroy us. It can't be accurate because the consequences are too cruel.' She wasn't arguing about the test methodology or the lab's error rate—she was dismissing the finding because accepting it would be painful. That's not how truth works.

cases.label: appeal_to_consequences
cases.argument: Consider the following line of reasoning. The city council's traffic study predicts that the new bypass will increase noise levels by 8 decibels in the residential corridor. If we accept this finding as true, property values will drop and the neighborhood will suffer. Therefore, the traffic study must be flawed. This argument evaluates the truth of the study's prediction based entirely on the desirability of its implications. A study's accuracy does not depend on whether we like what it says—a methodologically sound study can deliver unwelcome news.

cases.label: appeal_to_consequences
cases.argument: You: The employment data clearly shows that minimum wage increases in Seattle did not reduce job growth. That's what the University of Washington study found. Me: You have to understand, if that turns out to be true, then every small business lobbyist will use it to block wage protections in other cities. We can't let that happen. You're not engaging with the data—you're telling me we should reject the finding because accepting it would have bad political consequences down the line. The study is either well-designed or it isn't, regardless of who benefits from the result.

cases.label: appeal_to_motive
cases.argument: I agree with most of what she said about district rezoning—the overcrowding numbers are real and something needs to change. She's not making this proposal because the data supports it; she's making it because her kids benefit. The rezoning plan might still be the right one, but we can set it aside given where it's coming from.

cases.label: appeal_to_motive
cases.argument: Consider the Lancet paper by Nakamura et al. arguing that statin therapy should be discontinued for patients over 75. It is worth noting that Nakamura holds three patents on alternative cholesterol-management nutraceuticals and serves on the board of a supplement company that would directly gain market share if statins were deprescribed. The thrust of the paper can therefore be dismissed without further scrutiny. The argument's validity is set aside not on the basis of the data, but because the author stands to profit from its conclusion.

cases.label: appeal_to_motive
cases.argument: At the community board meeting last Tuesday, the landlord on Cedar Street argued that rent stabilization hurts tenants because it discourages property maintenance. He spoke for ten minutes with charts and everything. Then someone pointed out he owns twelve buildings in the neighborhood. A tenant stood up and said, 'Of course he says rent control is bad—he collects the rent checks. We don't need to address his argument because why he's making it is obvious.' The room applauded and moved on. But the charts were still sitting there, unanswered.

cases.label: two_wrongs
cases.argument: You: The contract says you can't share client data with third parties, and you forwarded the spreadsheet to your subcontractor without permission. That's a breach. Me: Are you serious? Your sales team takes prospects out to dinners worth hundreds of dollars and calls it 'relationship building' when the employee handbook caps gifts at fifty. If the company ignores one rule when it's convenient, I don't see why I'm getting singled out for bending another. Everyone cuts corners around here.

cases.label: two_wrongs
cases.argument: Consider the EU's complaint against Google for favoring its own shopping service in search results. The defense offered is straightforward: Amazon does the same thing on its platform—search for any product and Amazon's private-label version appears first. The reasoning here does not establish that Google's conduct is lawful; it merely notes that others break the same rule unpunished.

cases.label: two_wrongs
cases.argument: In the peer code review, I flagged that the authentication module was storing session tokens in localStorage, which is vulnerable to XSS.
