The lead architect even approved that PR. If it was a real problem, someone would have flagged it back then.' Whether a security vulnerability was missed in the past has no bearing on whether the current code should follow the standard today.

cases.label: ad_hominem_circumstantial
cases.argument: The DevOps team is recommending we migrate from our self-hosted GitLab instance to GitHub Enterprise. cases.argument: Consider the following reasoning about why deregulation stimulates growth. When government removes unnecessary bureaucratic constraints on business, the economy expands because the private sector is freed from the artificial barriers that inhibit productive activity. The argument simply assumes that the removed regulations are 'unnecessary' and 'artificial barriers'—which is exactly the point in dispute. The conclusion that deregulation stimulates growth follows only if we have already accepted that the regulations serve no legitimate purpose.

cases.label: begging_the_question
cases.argument: The hiring committee met for three hours on Thursday. The chair opened by saying we need to find a candidate who fits the company culture, because cultural fit is essential for team cohesion. By the end, they had rejected the two most qualified applicants. When I asked why, the chair said both lacked cultural fit. I asked what that meant specifically, and she said they wouldn't integrate well with the team. The reasoning was a closed loop—the candidates were rejected for lacking something that was defined only by the fact that they'd been rejected for lacking it.

cases.label: begging_the_question
cases.argument: You: Why do you say the new encryption standard is secure? Me: Because it was designed by people who understand cryptography. You: But how do we know they produced a secure algorithm? Me: Well, they wouldn't have implemented it if it weren't secure. You're just restating the conclusion as a premise. The claim that the algorithm is secure is being defended by the claim that the designers only produce secure algorithms—which assumes the very thing you're trying to establish about this specific algorithm.

cases.label: loaded_question

The attending had not agreed that the delay caused the deterioration—that was the central question under review. But the way the question was framed, answering it required accepting the causal link as established fact. The attending could not describe protocol changes without implicitly conceding the premise.

cases.label: loaded_question
cases.argument: At Wednesday's press briefing, a journalist asked the police commissioner, 'Now that body camera footage has confirmed the department's pattern of escalating routine traffic stops, how do you plan to retrain officers?' The commissioner froze for a moment. The footage in question was from a single incident and was still under internal review—no pattern had been established. But denying the premise meant looking evasive, and answering meant accepting that a pattern existed. The question itself built in the conclusion the journalist wanted.

cases.label: loaded_question
cases.argument: You: How long did you know the quarterly projections were inflated before you notified the audit committee? Me: I'm not going to answer that—the premise of your question is that the projections were inflated, and that hasn't been established. You: So you're refusing to say when you found out? Me: I'm refusing to accept the framing. You're asking 'when' as if the fact of inflation is settled. That's the exact thing the audit is investigating. You can't package the conclusion as a timestamp question and demand I fill in the date.

But that's exactly the point—no study has demonstrated it's safe either. In the absence of definitive proof of harmlessness, we should assume the preservative is dangerous and remove it from the food supply. The burden here is being placed on the wrong side of the evidence gap. A lack of proof of safety is being treated as proof of danger.

cases.label: appeal_to_ignorance
cases.argument: My neighbor swears her house is haunted. She's been telling everyone in the neighborhood for years. The interesting thing is that she's invited three different paranormal investigation teams, and not one of them has been able to debunk her claims. No one can explain the cold spots or the sounds she records. She treats this as confirmation—if the experts can't prove it's not a ghost, then it probably is. But the inability to disprove a supernatural explanation doesn't make it any more likely than a loose pipe or a drafty window.

cases.label: appeal_to_ignorance
cases.argument: In the pipeline integrity review, three independent engineering firms examined the weld radiographs from the 2018 construction phase and found no evidence of hydrogen-induced cracking. Since no defect has been found after exhaustive non-destructive testing, we can treat the welds as sound and proceed with the pressure increase. The absence of evidence of cracking is being converted into evidence of absence—but NDT has known sensitivity limits, and micro-cracks below the detection threshold remain a genuine possibility that is not ruled out by a null finding.

Show me the electrical inspection reports. You made the claim that it's not arson, so it's on you to back that up. Wait, no—I'm the one claiming arson. I'm the one asserting a crime occurred. The person making the positive claim carries the burden. I can't just toss the assertion out there and demand you disprove it.

cases.label: shifting_burden_of_proof
cases.argument: During the town hall about the proposed cellular tower, a resident stood up and declared that the radio frequency emissions cause childhood leukemia. The planning commissioner asked if she had a study. She didn't, but she told the commissioner, 'You're the one who wants to build this thing next to a school—you prove to me that it's safe. Show me the long-term pediatric exposure data. Until you do, this tower doesn't go up.' The claim was hers, but the demand for evidence was placed entirely on the other side.

cases.label: shifting_burden_of_proof
cases.argument: Consider the assertion that the artifact recovered from the excavation site is a forgery planted by a rival archaeology team. The claim is advanced without documentation—no receipt, no whistleblower, no forensic report. Yet when challenged, the proponent shifts the ground: 'Prove that it isn't a forgery. Show me the provenance chain. If you can't establish complete provenance, then forgery is the default assumption.' This inverts the normal epistemic order. The positive claim of forgery requires support; a gap in provenance does not make forgery the null hypothesis.

cases.argument: The Annals of the Four Masters is the principal chronicle of medieval Irish history, covering events from the Deluge to 1616 CE. There is no entry in the Annals for a large-scale Viking settlement at what is now Limerick city prior to 922 CE. If such a settlement had existed, the annalists would certainly have recorded it, given their meticulous documentation of Norse activity elsewhere. Therefore, we can conclude that Limerick was not a significant Viking site before 922. This inference turns on whether the annalists' silence is truly probative.

cases.label: argument_from_silence

Nobody mentioned the database migration script even once. Not during standup, not in code review, not in the retrospective notes. If the migration had actually been run before the launch, someone on a team of fourteen engineers would have referenced it somewhere in those thousands of messages. The fact that it never comes up means it simply didn't happen.

cases.label: argument_from_silence
cases.argument: You: The board must have approved the acquisition in their December meeting. Me: But the December minutes are twenty-three pages long and don't mention the acquisition once. You: Exactly—if there had been any objection or controversy, it would be in the minutes. The silence means the vote was unanimous and routine. Me: That's a wild inference. Maybe the discussion happened in executive session and the minutes were sealed. Or maybe it was tabled entirely. The absence of a mention doesn't tell you which of several possible things happened.

Inflation is the economy's natural response to an underlying imbalance — excessive money supply chasing constrained goods. Raising interest rates to suppress inflation is like prescribing antipyretics and discharging the patient. We should be addressing root causes: supply chain fragility, energy dependence, and housing undersupply. The analogy holds because both are systemic responses that get mistaken for the disease itself.

The word pivots from a narrow technical sense in the premise to a colloquial sense in the conclusion.

He was treating a grammatically ambiguous sentence as if it had only one possible reading and using that reading to discipline people.

cases.label: amphiboly
cases.argument: Consider the following agreement clause: 'The consultant shall provide services to the client and its affiliates covered by this agreement.' A dispute arose over whether 'covered by this agreement' modifies only 'affiliates' or both 'the client' and 'its affiliates.' If it modifies both, the consultant's obligations to the client itself are bounded by the scope clause. If only affiliates, the obligations to the client are unlimited. The consultant argued the plain text clearly supports the broader obligation, since 'covered by this agreement' naturally attaches to the nearest noun phrase. But the grammar is genuinely ambiguous — the sentence supports both parses equally. The conclusion about the scope of obligation only appears to follow because one syntactic reading is silently preferred over the other.

cases.label: amphiboly
cases.argument: I agree with most of what the policy committee put forward. The data retention rules make sense, and the new security protocols are overdue. But the section that says 'employees must report to their supervisor any violation of company policy they witness' is being read by HR as requiring self-reporting — meaning if you break a policy yourself, you must report it too. The sentence structure allows that reading, since 'they witness' could refer back to 'employees' as the witnesses, not restrict violations to those committed by others. They're using that reading to fire people who didn't self-report minor infractions, claiming the language is clear. But nobody reading that sentence in good faith would think it compels you to turn yourself in.

cases.label: accent
cases.argument: 'I read your review of my proposal. You said it was ambitious and well-researched.' 'I also said the timeline was unrealistic and the budget projections were incomplete. You can't just quote the complimentary part and ignore the rest.' 'But you did say it was ambitious and well-researched — those were your words. So you agree the proposal has merit.' 'I said far more than that. You're selectively quoting to create the impression that I endorsed it.' 'I'm quoting you directly. I'm not fabricating anything. Your own published words say positive things about the proposal. If you didn't mean it, you shouldn't have written it.' 'That's not how a full review works. The overall assessment was negative.' 'The overall assessment isn't what I'm citing. I'm citing the part where you praised it.'

cases.label: accent
cases.argument: The film critic's full review reads: 'If you can overlook the wooden acting and implausible plot twists, this film is a masterpiece of visual storytelling.' The studio's ad campaign quotes: '...a masterpiece of visual storytelling — The Times.' By excising the conditional clause and isolating the compliment, the fragment is made to express the opposite of what the original communicated. A reader encountering only the ad copy would reasonably conclude the critic gave a positive review, when in fact the full sentence was a qualified dismissal. The argument that the critic endorsed the film turns entirely on where the emphasis falls in a selectively truncated quotation.

cases.label: accent
cases.argument: So I was watching the mayoral debate, and the challenger asked the incumbent whether she supported defunding the police. She said, 'I do not believe that reducing the police budget is the right approach at this time.' The next morning, the challenger's campaign put out a mailer with her photo and the headline: 'Mayor says reducing the police budget is the right approach.' They had dropped the words 'I do not believe that' and the trailing qualifier, flipping the meaning of her statement entirely. They didn't change any words — they just omitted the ones that supplied the negation — and presented the resulting fragment as her quote. The emphasis shifted from refusal to endorsement by selective omission.

cases.label: definist
cases.argument: At the town hall, the developer argued his project wouldn't cause any environmental damage. When an environmental scientist asked how he defined the term, he explained: 'Environmental damage means an irreversible change to the ecosystem. Our project will alter some wetlands temporarily, but the mitigation plan restores the area after construction. Since the change is reversible, there is no environmental damage — by definition.' He built his conclusion into the definition itself by choosing a meaning of 'damage' that excludes the entire category of temporary harm. Under any standard ecological definition, filling wetlands for two years would count as damage regardless of eventual restoration, but his definition settled the dispute before it began.

cases.label: definist
cases.argument: Define a 'tax increase' as an increase in the statutory tax rate that applies to a given income bracket. Given that definition, eliminating mortgage-interest deductions and the child tax credit while leaving the marginal rates unchanged is not, by definition, a tax increase. Therefore, this tax reform legislation contains no tax increases whatsoever. The reasoning is internally consistent given the definition, but the definition is loaded — it excludes any change in effective tax burden, which is what economists and ordinary taxpayers mean by a tax increase. The conclusion that 'taxes are not going up' is built into the choice of definition rather than derived from neutral premises.

cases.label: definist
cases.argument: 'Your company discriminates against older workers.' 'No, we don't. Age discrimination means treating someone differently because of their age, with intent to harm them on that basis.' 'Right. And you laid off everyone over fifty in the restructuring.' 'We laid off people in redundant positions. It's not age discrimination because discrimination requires malicious intent to harm someone because of their age. We restructured for operational efficiency — there was no prejudicial motive.' 'That's not what discrimination means under the law. Disparate impact counts.' 'Of course it doesn't. Discrimination is an intentional act of prejudice. Since we had no prejudicial intent, it can't be discrimination. By definition.'

cases.label: composition
cases.argument: Our basketball team recruited five transfers this offseason, and every single one of them shot over forty percent from three-point range last year at their previous schools. Each guy is a proven perimeter threat. So we are going to be the best three-point shooting team in the conference — there's no way around it. When every player on the floor can knock down threes at an elite clip, the team as a whole becomes an unstoppable outside shooting machine. You can't guard five snipers at once. Defenses will have to pick their poison, and we'll rain threes all night.

cases.label: composition
cases.argument: Every stock in this mutual fund has individually outperformed the S&P 500 over the past five years. Each holding is a proven market-beater on its own. Therefore, the fund as a whole is guaranteed to beat the market going forward. If every component outperforms the benchmark, the aggregate portfolio must outperform the benchmark — that's just arithmetic. The fund's weighted average return across all positions will necessarily exceed the index return, since each position contributes positively to the total.

cases.label: composition
cases.argument: Consider any chain of deductive reasoning. Each inferential step in the chain is individually plausible — let's assign it an 85% chance of being correct. There are eight steps in total. Since each link in the argument is solid on its own, the chain as a whole must be roughly 85% likely to yield a true conclusion. The strength of the overall argument equals the average strength of its component inferences. Anything less would imply that good reasoning, when combined, somehow becomes bad reasoning — which is absurd.

cases.label: division
cases.argument: My roommate signed up at this gym because he heard it produces the most competitive powerlifters in the region. I asked what that has to do with his personal training sessions, and he said, 'This gym is elite. How could an elite gym not have elite personal trainers? The quality of a gym is the quality of its staff. So whoever I get assigned to will be top-tier.' He got matched with a trainer who'd been certified two weeks earlier and injured his rotator cuff within a month. The gym's reputation came from a handful of world-class coaches who trained the competitive team — not from the general personal training staff.

cases.label: division
cases.argument: Google has the most capable engineering organization on the planet — their products serve billions of users, they solve distributed systems problems at unprecedented scale, and they consistently ship innovations that reshape entire industries. So any individual software engineer at Google must be exceptional. If I'm hiring and I see Google on a resume, I don't even need to interview them. The organization is world-class, which means its individual engineers are world-class. You can't have a great engineering culture without great engineers filling every seat.

cases.label: division
cases.argument: 'This university has a top-five computer science program. So every CS professor here must be a top-tier researcher.' 'That doesn't follow. A great department can have a few stars and some mediocre faculty — the ranking doesn't mean every single professor is elite.' 'How is that possible? The department's ranking comes from its faculty's aggregate research output and citation counts. If the whole department is excellent, then each contributing member must be excellent individually. The overall quality is just the sum of the individual qualities.' 'Not really — a handful of highly-cited researchers can carry the numbers while others publish very little.' 'That makes no sense. The whole is made of its parts.'

cases.label: ecological_fallacy
cases.argument: My uncle was visiting for the weekend and noticed our neighborhood has a lot of Asian-American families. He said, 'You must have great math tutors around here — Asian students score the highest in math on average, it's a well-known fact.' I mentioned that our neighbor's son is Asian-American and really struggles with algebra, barely passing. My uncle waved it off: 'That can't be right. The statistics don't lie — Asian students as a group consistently test the highest in mathematics. There must be some mistake in his grades, or maybe he's not applying himself.' He applied a group-level statistic to an individual kid as if the average described every member of the group.

cases.label: ecological_fallacy
cases.argument: The county health department's latest epidemiology report shows that zip code 90210 has the highest life expectancy in the region at 85.4 years. Mrs. Patterson resides in 90210. Based on these population-level data, her predicted lifespan is approximately 85 years. The reasoning is straightforward: her zip code's life expectancy is the best available estimate of her individual life expectancy, since she belongs to the population from which the statistic was calculated. The group average reliably describes each of its members.

cases.label: ecological_fallacy
cases.argument: I appreciate the thoroughness of your demographic analysis, and I agree that neighborhoods with higher median incomes do show better cardiovascular health outcomes on average. But you can't turn around and tell Mr. Garcia — who lives in one of those neighborhoods — that his personal heart disease risk is low because his zip code's average is good. He works a high-stress sixty-hour week, smokes a pack a day, hasn't seen a doctor in five years, and eats fast food for most meals. His individual risk profile looks nothing like his neighborhood's average. The ecological data describes the group, not the man.

cases.label: affirming_the_consequent
cases.argument: My neighbor's car wouldn't start yesterday morning. He popped the hood, wiggled the battery cables, and said, 'If the battery is dead, the car won't start. Well, the car won't start. So the battery must be dead — that's the problem.' He walked half a mile to the auto parts store, bought a new battery for a hundred and eighty dollars, installed it himself in the driveway, turned the key, and nothing. Same silence. Turned out the starter motor had seized. The car not starting was consistent with a dead battery, but it was also consistent with a dozen other mechanical failures. He saw the expected outcome and concluded the single cause he had in mind must be the one.

cases.label: affirming_the_consequent
cases.argument: 'If she's angry with me, she won't return my calls. She hasn't returned any of my calls all week. So she's definitely angry with me — the evidence is right there.' 'Or her phone is broken. Or she's traveling internationally. Or she's swamped at work and not returning anyone's calls.' 'Those are all possible, but not returning calls is exactly what happens when someone is upset. I predicted the outcome, and the outcome happened. That confirms the condition. You're reaching for alternative explanations because you don't want to admit the obvious.' 'I'm saying there are multiple reasons someone might not call back.' 'And I'm saying the most logical one is anger, given the circumstances.'

cases.label: affirming_the_consequent
cases.argument: If a patient has acute appendicitis, they will present with right lower quadrant abdominal pain upon palpation. This patient presents with right lower quadrant abdominal pain. Therefore, this patient has acute appendicitis. Schedule the appendectomy. The reasoning follows a standard diagnostic pattern: the predicted clinical sign is observed, so the underlying condition is confirmed. While RLQ pain can also arise from ovarian cysts, kidney stones, mesenteric adenitis, or simple constipation, the presence of the expected symptom is taken as sufficient to establish the diagnosis without ruling out alternatives.

cases.label: denying_the_antecedent
cases.argument: My younger brother applied to an Ivy League school, and our dad told him throughout junior year, 'If you score above 1500 on the SAT, you'll get in. That's the ticket.' He ended up scoring a 1380. Our dad looked at the score report and said, 'Well, that settles it — you're not getting in. You didn't hit the number, so there's no chance. We should start looking at backup options now and not get your hopes up.' My brother got waitlisted and was eventually accepted in May, because admissions decisions depend on essays, extracurriculars, recommendations, and a dozen other factors — not just one test score. The SAT threshold was one route, not the only route.

cases.label: denying_the_antecedent
cases.argument: 'If we lower the price, sales volume will go up.' 'But we decided not to lower the price.' 'Exactly. So sales volume won't go up this quarter. We can forecast flat demand.' 'That doesn't follow at all. Sales could go up because of the new advertising campaign we just launched, or the holiday shopping season, or our main competitor just raised their prices by fifteen percent.' 'Those are speculative. The most direct lever on demand is price. Without a price reduction in place, a sales increase doesn't make sense.' 'You're treating price as if it's the only factor that affects consumer demand. It's not.'

cases.label: denying_the_antecedent
cases.argument: I'll grant you that consistent exercise is highly effective for weight loss. If someone maintains a regular workout routine — say, four sessions a week of moderate to vigorous activity — they will see a meaningful reduction in body weight over several months. But my brother doesn't exercise at all and never has. He's a sedentary person by nature. So he's not going to lose any weight. That's just the reality of the situation. He might try dieting or intermittent fasting here and there, but without the exercise piece being in place, the weight isn't coming off. People who don't exercise simply don't lose weight.

cases.label: loaded_language
cases.argument: So the planning commission held a hearing on the new apartment complex. The developer walked everyone through the floor plans, the traffic study, the 200 units of housing. Then the head of the neighborhood association stood up and said, "What we're actually being asked to approve is a concrete eyesore that will cram transients into matchbox units and choke our streets with congestion." No one asked about setbacks or parking ratios after that. The room voted it down.

cases.label: loaded_language
cases.argument: Let's assess the policy on its merits. The proposal would allow businesses to deduct entertainment expenses at 50%. The opposition has labeled this a "boondoggle for fat-cat executives to wine and dine on the public dime." That framing carries the conclusion before any numbers are examined — the word "boondoggle" assumes waste, "fat-cat" assumes undeserving recipients, and "public dime" assumes the deduction is a handout rather than a tax expenditure whose effect on investment should be measured against revenue forgone.

cases.label: loaded_language
cases.argument: "You're telling me the corporate restructuring plan makes sense?" "It does. The spin-off creates two focused businesses with clearer accountability." "Come on, this is textbook looting. They'll strip the profitable assets, load the husk with debt, and walk away with golden parachutes while the workers get pink slips." "You haven't read the term sheet." "Don't need to. I've seen this predator-capitalism playbook before."

cases.label: weasel_words
cases.argument: I attended a school board meeting where a parent argued against updating the science curriculum. She claimed "research shows that hands-on learning actually produces worse test outcomes than direct instruction." Another parent asked which research. She said "there are multiple studies" and moved on. Later, someone brought up a meta-analysis of over two hundred studies showing the opposite, but by then the board had already voted to table the curriculum update indefinitely.

cases.label: weasel_words
cases.argument: The case for eliminating remote work rests on three pillars: collaboration, culture, and productivity. On the productivity question, it is widely acknowledged that in-office teams outperform distributed ones on complex projects. Many experts agree that spontaneous interactions cannot be replicated digitally. And data indicates that mentoring relationships suffer significantly when junior staff work remotely. These claims — widely acknowledged, many experts, data indicates — are presented as settled conclusions without specifying which studies or whose expert opinion backs them.

cases.label: weasel_words
cases.argument: A position paper on post-operative recovery protocols argues that patients should be mobilized within six hours of abdominal surgery. "It is well known that early ambulation reduces the incidence of deep vein thrombosis," the paper states. "Most surgeons concur that prolonged bedrest carries significant risk." But the paper cites no trial, no cohort study, and no registry data. The phrases "it is well known" and "most surgeons concur" are doing the work that a systematic review or randomized controlled trial would be expected to supply in a clinical guideline.

cases.label: continuum_fallacy
cases.argument: My roommate argued we shouldn't bother recycling at all. He said, "If you actually trace it, our recycling goes to a facility that rejects about twenty percent of it because of contamination. So why do we draw the line at twenty percent? Why not nineteen? Why not twenty-one? There's no principled cutoff, so the whole enterprise is arbitrary." By that logic, he concluded, we might as well toss everything in the trash, because you can't define exactly how clean a batch has to be to count as "recycled."

cases.label: continuum_fallacy
cases.argument: "Obesity needs to be treated as a chronic disease." "There's no objective boundary between overweight and obese. The BMI cutoff of 30 is arbitrary — plenty of people at 29.9 have worse metabolic health than someone at 30.1. You can't point to a single extra pound that makes someone suddenly diseased." "So you're saying obesity doesn't exist?" "If you can't tell me exactly where it starts, then obesity is just a label we made up."

cases.label: continuum_fallacy
cases.argument: A defense attorney argues, "The statute criminalizes possession of a 'significant' quantity of a controlled substance. But the legislature never defined 'significant' in grams. Is five grams significant? Four? Four and a half? If the state cannot articulate the exact threshold, then 'significant' is unconstitutionally vague, and no conviction can ever rest on it." This reasoning treats the failure to specify a precise cutoff as proof that no meaningful threshold exists at all, ignoring that a kilogram is clearly significant and a trace residue clearly is not.

cases.label: reification
cases.argument: At the investor call, the CFO explained the layoffs like this: "The market has been demanding leaner operations, and the market doesn't wait. The market voted with its feet after our last earnings miss." He talked about the market the way you'd talk about a boss who could fire you. But a market isn't a person with a demand — it's thousands of unrelated trades whose aggregate went down. The board made a decision, then dressed it up as an impersonal force.

cases.label: reification
cases.argument: The claim that "nature selects for traits that maximize reproductive fitness" is routinely presented as if nature possesses foresight and intentionality. But natural selection is the differential survival of organisms with varying traits in a given environment — no agent is doing the selecting. Attributing purpose to "nature" smuggles in a teleology that the mechanism doesn't warrant, and it leads people to ask what nature "wants" instead of what environmental pressures actually operate.

cases.label: reification
cases.argument: Our architecture team keeps saying "the algorithm prefers sparse representations." The algorithm doesn't prefer anything. It computes a weighted sum of activations against a regularization penalty. The objective function happens to yield sparse weight matrices under L1 regularization. Talking about what the algorithm "prefers" makes it sound like it has taste or judgment. When you reify the math into an agent with preferences, junior engineers stop questioning the loss function and start treating the output as if it made a considered choice.

cases.label: base_rate_neglect
cases.argument: A friend took one of those direct-to-consumer genetic tests. It came back positive for a marker associated with a rare neurological condition — lifetime prevalence about one in ten thousand. She panicked, booked a neurologist, started reading caregiver support forums. The test company's own documentation says the false positive rate is around two percent. With a one-in-ten-thousand base rate, a two percent false positive rate means her odds of actually having the condition are about one in two hundred. But the word "positive" was all she could see.

cases.label: base_rate_neglect
cases.argument: A hiring manager reviews a candidate assessment. The profile warns that the candidate scored in the bottom decile on a conscientiousness inventory that correctly identifies low-conscientiousness employees eighty percent of the time. The manager concludes the candidate is almost certainly unreliable. However, if genuinely low-conscientiousness people are only five percent of the applicant pool, then even with eighty percent accuracy the probability that a flagged candidate is actually low in conscientiousness is under eighteen percent — lower than a coin flip's chance of being wrong.

cases.label: base_rate_neglect
cases.argument: "We should install that AI fraud detection system. It catches ninety-five percent of fraudulent transactions." "And its false positive rate?" "Three percent. That's excellent." "Three percent of millions of transactions is tens of thousands of legitimate purchases flagged as fraud every month. Given that actual fraud is maybe a tenth of a percent of all transactions, almost every alert your system generates is going to be a false alarm. Your fraud team will spend all its time chasing ghosts."

cases.label: gamblers_fallacy
cases.argument: My uncle plays the same lottery numbers every week: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42. Last month he switched them. He told me, "Those numbers haven't come up in twelve years. They're overdue. But I've been thinking — if they're overdue, that also means they're cursed. So I'm switching to ones that have come up recently. A hot streak is more reliable than waiting for the universe to balance the books." He genuinely believes the ping-pong balls know his birthday.

cases.label: gamblers_fallacy
cases.argument: A trader notes that the S&P 500 has closed lower for five consecutive sessions. The probability of six consecutive down days under random walk assumptions is roughly 1.6 percent. The trader therefore allocates heavily to a long position, reasoning that the streak is statistically unlikely to continue. This reasoning treats the daily close as drawing from a finite deck rather than as independent draws from a roughly stationary distribution. The market does not owe a green candle.

cases.label: gamblers_fallacy
cases.argument: I agree that in a fair casino, roulette spins are independent. No argument there. But this isn't a casino — it's a manufacturing line. We've had zero defects for eighteen batches straight. Statistically, we're due for a bad one. The process hasn't changed, but probability can't keep bending in our favor forever. I'm scheduling a full maintenance check before batch nineteen. Yes, the events are technically independent, but at some point you have to respect the odds catching up.

cases.label: regression_fallacy
cases.argument: Our sales rep had a terrible Q2 — worst numbers in the region. The VP put her on a performance improvement plan with weekly coaching calls. By Q4, her numbers were back to the regional average, and the VP presented this at the all-hands as proof that performance improvement plans work. But the worst quarter in the region is almost always followed by a better one, regardless of intervention. Someone had to be at the bottom, and whoever it was would have regressed toward the mean on their own.

cases.label: regression_fallacy
cases.argument: A clinic adopts a new antihypertensive protocol and publishes a case series: ten patients with markedly elevated systolic blood pressure at baseline showed a mean reduction of 28 mmHg after six weeks on the protocol. The authors attribute this to the novel agent combination. But the patients were selected precisely because their baseline readings were extreme — a classic regression-to-the-mean scenario. Without a control group, the observed improvement is confounded with the statistical tendency of extreme measurements to move closer to the population mean on remeasurement.

cases.label: regression_fallacy
cases.argument: "Ever since we started the after-school tutoring, Carl's grades have shot up." "He got a D on the midterm and now he's getting B's. That's great. But the D was his lowest grade in two years. If you pick a student at their worst moment and give them anything — tutoring, a pep talk, a new backpack — their next score will almost always be better. That's just the numbers settling back." "So you're saying tutoring does nothing?" "No, I'm saying you can't credit it without comparing to students who didn't get it."

cases.label: survivorship_bias
cases.argument: A tech blogger interviewed twelve startup founders who made it and wrote a book distilling their common habits: they all woke up before 6 AM, exercised daily, cold-emailed investors relentlessly. The thesis: do these three things and you'll build a unicorn. But the blogger never interviewed the hundred founders who woke up at 5 AM, ran marathons, sent thousands of cold emails, and still went bankrupt. The habits might be universal among startup founders, period. The ones who failed just aren't around to survey.

cases.label: survivorship_bias
cases.argument: An analysis of Fortune 500 companies finds that eighty-three percent underwent a major strategic pivot in their first five years. The consulting report concludes that pivoting early is a key driver of corporate longevity. This conclusion is drawn entirely from the survivors. Companies that pivoted and perished — and companies that never pivoted and perished — are absent from the sample. Without base rates on pivot outcomes, the correlation among survivors tells us nothing about the causal effect of pivoting on survival.

cases.label: survivorship_bias
cases.argument: A mutual fund prospectus highlights a ten-year track record showing the fund has beaten its benchmark by 2.3 percent annualized. The marketing copy frames this as evidence of superior stock-picking skill. What isn't mentioned: the fund family launched eighteen other funds in the same year, closed fourteen of them after underperformance, and merged the remaining assets into this survivor. The prospectus is studying the one coin that came up heads ten times in a row after flipping twenty coins and burying the tails.

cases.label: texas_sharpshooter
cases.argument: After the election, a pundit posted a map showing that counties along the I-35 corridor had swung toward the winner by an average of four points. He declared the "I-35 Realignment" a new political phenomenon and spent an hour on cable news explaining the cultural geography of interstate-adjacent voters. What he didn't mention: he looked at dozens of corridors — I-95, I-5, the Mississippi River counties, the Appalachian spine — and only the I-35 cluster showed a pattern worth talking about. The rest were noise.

cases.label: texas_sharpshooter
cases.argument: A researcher examines cancer incidence across 3,000 census tracts and identifies a cluster of twelve adjacent tracts with elevated rates of a rare leukemia. The cluster is statistically significant at p < 0.01 after Bonferroni correction, and the researcher hypothesizes an environmental exposure from a nearby industrial site. The problem: the researcher tested over a hundred spatial partitions and exposure radii before finding this one. The p-value assumes a single pre-specified hypothesis. The effective degrees of freedom consumed by the search are unaccounted for.

cases.label: texas_sharpshooter
cases.argument: I'll grant you that the cancer cluster looks alarming on the map, and I understand why residents are scared. But here's the thing. The health department didn't set out to study that specific four-block radius. They mapped every address in a city of two million, tried a dozen different clustering algorithms, adjusted for age, occupation, and water source in multiple model specifications, and then reported the one combination that yielded a significant result. When you fire enough statistical bullets and then draw a bullseye around the one that hit, you haven't discovered a target. You've just described where a bullet landed.

cases.label: VALID

On his last three projects in this county, the permits office has records of him failing final electrical inspection twice and having a lien filed against him by a subcontractor he never paid. This isn't about whether I like the guy. I've never met him. The license board documents are the evidence, and they show a pattern of work that doesn't meet code. When the question is whether to hand someone a fifty-thousand-dollar renovation, their actual track record of doing the job correctly is the most relevant information available. That's due diligence, not a personal attack.

cases.label: VALID

Independent traffic studies are standard for variances of this scale, and using the same consultant for both creates a structural conflict. The firm has every incentive to produce traffic numbers that support the project their client wants approved. I'm not saying the numbers are automatically wrong because of who prepared them. I'm saying the study lacks the independence our ordinance requires, and the vote should be postponed until the city engineer can commission a separate review.

cases.label: VALID
cases.argument: The lease agreement says I have until the end of business Friday to either sign the renewal at the stated rate increase of eight percent or vacate the premises. I asked about a month-to-month holdover at the current rate, a shorter renewal term, or negotiating the increase down to five percent. The property manager said the owner has standing instructions to accept only signed two-year renewals at the listed rate. Those really are my two options under this contract: sign or leave. It's not a false dilemma when the counterparty controls the available choices and has clearly ruled out everything else.

cases.label: VALID
cases.argument: The wage stagnation in manufacturing over the last two decades maps almost perfectly onto the decline in private-sector union density over the same period, and the mechanism is well-documented: without collective bargaining, individual workers lose the leverage to capture productivity gains as wage increases. Comparing these two trends and treating them as causally linked isn't a false equivalence. It's the central finding of a substantial body of labor economics research by authors ranging from Card to Farber. The comparison holds because union density directly affects the bargaining power that drives wage outcomes.

cases.label: VALID
cases.argument: Switching the municipal vehicle fleet to electric would eliminate roughly twenty-four hundred metric tons of CO2 equivalent annually based on our current fleet mileage and the regional grid's generation mix. After that, the city saves approximately four hundred thousand dollars annually compared to maintaining the existing combustion fleet. The main risk is battery replacement cost in years ten through twelve, which should be budgeted as a separate capital reserve starting in year five.

cases.label: VALID
cases.argument: The decision to move our database hosting from on-premise servers to a managed cloud provider came down to three factors that outweighed the higher monthly operating cost. First, our in-house DBA team has shrunk from three people to one, and recruiting replacements has taken over six months per position. Second, our last unplanned outage lasted eleven hours because the on-call engineer couldn't diagnose a RAID controller failure remotely. Third, the cloud provider's SOC 2 Type II certification satisfies the compliance requirements our enterprise customers have been demanding in RFPs. The cost difference is real at about eighteen percent more per year, but the reliability and compliance gains make it the right tradeoff.

cases.label: VALID
cases.argument: In 2019, the county approved a variance to let a single developer build a condo complex two stories above the existing height limit. Property values in adjacent residential blocks dropped twelve percent as views were blocked and parking congestion worsened. The legal concept of precedent is not hypothetical here — zoning boards are bound by consistency requirements, and this board's own public minutes from the subsequent hearings explicitly cite the original variance as the basis for each approval. The causal chain from that first exception to the neighborhood impact is documented, not speculative.

cases.label: VALID
cases.argument: Across the forty-seven stores where we piloted the new inventory management system, same-store sales grew 3.8% in the following quarter. Stores in the control group, matched on size, location, and historical performance, showed 0.2% growth over the same period. The p-value on the difference is 0.003. We controlled for seasonality, staffing changes, and local promotions. The system reduced stockouts by 22% and overstock by 18% — both independently verified through cycle counts. This is not coincidence or a small-sample artifact; it is a controlled quasi-experiment with a large enough sample to support the conclusion that the system caused the improvement.
